MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=1] A(1): The paper provides no quantitative comparisons to any competitive baselines (summary states “none mentioned”), so it fails to situate performance relative to prior relation‑extraction or QA‑based methods. While related work is reportedly discussed thoroughly, the absence of controlled baseline experiments prevents any fair assessment of gains or tradeoffs, and the paper therefore does not analyze differences or acknowledge when prior methods match or exceed its performance. From a rigor standpoint this omission is fatal for claims of improved or zero‑shot generalization. | B(1): According to the provided summary the paper does not report comparisons against any baselines — no recent relation-extraction or QA-as-RE systems are listed — so it fails to situate empirical performance relative to prior methods. While the related work is described as thorough, absence of quantitative baseline comparisons means we cannot judge whether the gains are due to the modeling choice, data scale, or distant supervision, which is a critical omission for practical evaluation and adoption.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides no quantitative comparisons to any competitive baselines (summary states “none mentioned”), so it fails to situate performance relative to prior relation‑extraction or QA‑based methods. While related work is reportedly discussed thoroughly, the absence of controlled baseline experiments prevents any fair assessment of gains or tradeoffs, and the paper therefore does not analyze differences or acknowledge when prior methods match or exceed its performance. From a rigor standpoint this omission is fatal for claims of improved or zero‑shot generalization.

reviewer_b.justification: According to the provided summary the paper does not report comparisons against any baselines — no recent relation-extraction or QA-as-RE systems are listed — so it fails to situate empirical performance relative to prior methods. While the related work is described as thorough, absence of quantitative baseline comparisons means we cannot judge whether the gains are due to the modeling choice, data scale, or distant supervision, which is a critical omission for practical evaluation and adoption.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The summary indicates the paper is well-organized (clear pipeline from reduction idea to model extension and large-scale data collection) and states main contributions early (mapping relations to QA, BiDAF extension, cheap distant-supervision dataset). Notation and experimental details appear adequately reported (hyperparameters given) and related work coverage is thorough, while figures/tables and examples are described as effective and writing quality is good. As a cautious reviewer I withhold top marks because the summary does not explicitly confirm that all notation is formally defined throughout or that every figure/table is fully self-contained, suggesting only minor presentation issues. | B(4): The paper is well organized and follows a clear logical flow from motivation through method (reduction to reading comprehension and BiDAF extension) to large-scale data creation and experiments, and the main contributions are stated early. Notation and model details are consistently presented (hyperparameters reported), and related work and datasets are covered thoroughly, which aids reproducibility. Figures, tables, and examples (e.g., question templates, error analyses) are generally effective and informative. A few minor presentation issues remain (e.g., explicit mention of released code/datasets is absent), but overall the writing is clear and easy to follow.

reviewer_a.justification: The summary indicates the paper is well-organized (clear pipeline from reduction idea to model extension and large-scale data collection) and states main contributions early (mapping relations to QA, BiDAF extension, cheap distant-supervision dataset). Notation and experimental details appear adequately reported (hyperparameters given) and related work coverage is thorough, while figures/tables and examples are described as effective and writing quality is good. As a cautious reviewer I withhold top marks because the summary does not explicitly confirm that all notation is formally defined throughout or that every figure/table is fully self-contained, suggesting only minor presentation issues.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized and follows a clear logical flow from motivation through method (reduction to reading comprehension and BiDAF extension) to large-scale data creation and experiments, and the main contributions are stated early. Notation and model details are consistently presented (hyperparameters reported), and related work and datasets are covered thoroughly, which aids reproducibility. Figures, tables, and examples (e.g., question templates, error analyses) are generally effective and informative. A few minor presentation issues remain (e.g., explicit mention of released code/datasets is absent), but overall the writing is clear and easy to follow.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper advances a notable and interpretable perspective shift by reducing relation extraction to reading-comprehension QA and operationalizing it via natural-language question templates and answerability modeling; this conceptual reframing is a clear differentiator from conventional supervised RE pipelines. The technical contributions (extending BiDAF for answerability and cheaply creating a huge distantly supervised dataset via schema querification) are useful but largely engineering extensions of existing neural QA and distant-supervision techniques rather than fundamentally new algorithms. From my conservative, theory-oriented vantage this is a notable novelty (practical and methodological), but it stops short of highly original, principled advances that would warrant the top score. | B(4): This paper offers a notable and practically useful reframing—treating relation extraction as reading-comprehension by mapping relation slots to natural-language question templates—which is a fresh perspective that simplifies downstream engineering and enables zero-shot extraction. The combination of this formulation with a modestly adapted BiDAF to handle answerability and a pragmatic, low-cost scheme (schema querification + distant supervision yielding 30M examples for $1,250) is a non-trivial, application-oriented contribution that would inform practitioners. While the model extension itself is incremental, the overall idea and the demonstrated scalable data-generation pipeline clearly differentiate the work from prior approaches and provide valuable, reproducible utility.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper advances a notable and interpretable perspective shift by reducing relation extraction to reading-comprehension QA and operationalizing it via natural-language question templates and answerability modeling; this conceptual reframing is a clear differentiator from conventional supervised RE pipelines. The technical contributions (extending BiDAF for answerability and cheaply creating a huge distantly supervised dataset via schema querification) are useful but largely engineering extensions of existing neural QA and distant-supervision techniques rather than fundamentally new algorithms. From my conservative, theory-oriented vantage this is a notable novelty (practical and methodological), but it stops short of highly original, principled advances that would warrant the top score.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper offers a notable and practically useful reframing—treating relation extraction as reading-comprehension by mapping relation slots to natural-language question templates—which is a fresh perspective that simplifies downstream engineering and enables zero-shot extraction. The combination of this formulation with a modestly adapted BiDAF to handle answerability and a pragmatic, low-cost scheme (schema querification + distant supervision yielding 30M examples for $1,250) is a non-trivial, application-oriented contribution that would inform practitioners. While the model extension itself is incremental, the overall idea and the demonstrated scalable data-generation pipeline clearly differentiate the work from prior approaches and provide valuable, reproducible utility.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the core idea is clear and plausible and supported by some experiments, but they also identify major methodological weaknesses: missing competitive baselines, lack of statistical testing/ablations, and insufficient characterization/verification of noisy distant supervision. These gaps materially weaken the paper’s claims about generalization and zero-shot performance, so while the approach is promising, the evidence is currently insufficient to justify stronger soundness/correctness.

reviewer_a.justification: While the paper clearly states its main idea (reducing relation extraction to reading-comprehension questions) and presents experiments showing generalization to new templates and some zero-shot capability, the experimental methodology is weak: no competitive baselines are reported and statistical testing/controlled ablations appear absent. The use of massive distantly-supervised data (30M examples for $1,250) is not accompanied by a rigorous noise characterization or sufficient human verification (persona requirement: <5% human-verified), undermining claims about training quality and robustness. Given these significant methodological gaps and unsupported assumptions about noisy supervision and lack of baselines, the conclusions are not fully justified despite promising empirical signals.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper states clear, testable claims and presents sensible methodology (reducing relation extraction to reading-comprehension, extending BiDAF to model answerability) and reports large-scale, low-cost data creation — all supported by experiments. However the experimental methodology appears incomplete: no mention of competitive baselines or statistical significance testing, and zero-shot results are acknowledged as substantially weaker, leaving some claims about generalization under-supported. There are also potential unsupported assumptions around distant supervision quality and evaluation penalizing reasonable span variations, which could materially affect reported precision/recall. Overall the technical approach is plausible and partially validated but missing key controls and comparisons needed to fully support the main claims.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper tackles an important practical problem and offers a compelling, low-cost QA-based reduction with clear potential to change practice. Reviewer A rightly flags limitations in baselines, noise characterization, and zero-shot accuracy that temper immediate transformative impact, while Reviewer B emphasizes the engineerable, widely usable contributions and likely uptake. Balancing these points, a score of 4 reflects strong practical impact and likely influence despite some empirical and robustness gaps that prevent a top score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an important practical problem—scalable relation extraction—and presents a compelling reduction to reading comprehension that enables cheap large-scale training (30M distant-supervision examples for $1,250) and demonstrated zero-shot extraction capability. However, from my robustness-focused perspective the impact is curtailed by the apparent lack of competitive baselines and rigorous noise analysis (no mention of human verification ≥5% or characterization of label noise), and lower accuracy on truly unseen relations limits immediate adoption. The approach opens promising directions for QA-based IE, but without stronger empirical controls, ablations, and theoretical or statistical guarantees its influence is likely moderate rather than transformative.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses an important practical problem by reframing relation extraction as reading-comprehension QA and demonstrating a straightforward, engineerable reduction that practitioners can adopt. The cheap large-scale dataset (30M examples for $1,250) and the extended BiDAF model for answerability materially lower the cost and complexity of training extractors, and the demonstrated generalization to new question templates and zero-shot relations suggests this will change how people build and scale RE systems. While zero-shot accuracy is lower and some evaluation limitations exist, the approach opens clear, usable directions for low-cost data generation and QA-based extraction that are likely to be widely built upon.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper offers an interesting, scalable framing and impressive large-scale distant-supervised training, but importantly lacks competitive baselines, key ablations/sensitivity analyses, and human/noise verification or clear code/data release for full reproducibility. Given the solid empirical effort that plausibly supports the main claim but the missing comparisons and analyses that limit causal conclusions and reliability, a middle score of 3 reflects useful contribution tempered by significant methodological gaps.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents an interesting framing and large-scale distant-supervised training (30M examples) and discusses limitations and error sources, but experimental support is shallow: no competitive baselines are reported and key controlled comparisons (e.g., ablations of the answerability module or sensitivity to question-template variability) are missing. Crucially, the distant supervision pipeline lacks the required noise analysis or human verification (I expect ≥5% human-checked examples) to justify reliability of the massive cheaply-built training set. Reproducibility is only partial (hyperparameters reported but code/dataset-release not mentioned), though related work coverage appears thorough.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a clear experimental setup (BiDAF extension for answerability), large-scale cheaply-produced training data (30M examples for $1,250), and evaluation on precision/recall/F1 including transfer to unseen relations — enough to credibly support the main reduction claim. However, no baselines are reported and ablations/sensitivity analyses are not mentioned in the summary, which weakens causal attribution of gains to key design choices (model variants, amount/quality of distant supervision, template variability). Related work coverage and discussion of limitations are said to be thorough, and hyperparameters are reported, but code/dataset release is not mentioned, so full reproducibility is uncertain. From a practical-NLP perspective this is a useful, scalable approach with solid empirical evidence, but missing comparisons and deeper analyses keep it from being fully comprehensive.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper tackles a highly relevant problem and presents a practically useful reduction of relation extraction to reading comprehension with promising large-scale data collection. Reviewer A’s concerns about missing competitive baselines, limited human validation of distant supervision, and lack of controlled splits/statistical testing suggest the empirical evaluation needs strengthening before top-tier publication. I therefore give a score reflecting strong topical fit and promising contribution but recommend revisions to address the methodological gaps.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a topical NLP/ML problem (relation extraction via QA) and uses methods and metrics expected at top venues, so it is broadly in scope. However, from the summary I am concerned about empirical rigor for this community: no competitive baselines are reported, the distant supervision construct appears unverified by human labels (violating my expectation of at least a small validated subset to characterize noise), and there is limited evidence of controlled difficulty splits or statistical testing. Given these methodological gaps, the work is borderline appropriate for a top-tier conference without stronger empirical controls and baselines.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it tackles a core NLP problem (relation extraction) but reframes it in a practically useful way (reduction to reading comprehension) and demonstrates large-scale, low-cost data creation (≈30M examples for $1,250) that will interest applied researchers. The method extends well-known neural RC models (BiDAF with answerability) and evaluates with standard metrics (precision/recall/F1) on realistic resources (WikiReading/Wikidata/SQuAD variants), showing useful zero-shot transfer — exactly the kind of scalable, reproducible, and application-oriented work valued by the community. Given the clear relevance to relation extraction, QA, and dataset construction, the paper addresses the right audience and is positioned appropriately for a top conference.

main_claims: Relation extraction can be reduced to answering reading-comprehension questions by associating one or more natural-language questions with each relation slot.
This reduction enables training high-quality relation-extraction models by extending recent neural reading-comprehension techniques (they extend BiDAF with variables to model answerability).
Large training sets can be built cheaply via schema querification combined with distant supervision (they generated 30,000,000 examples for $1,250).
The approach generalizes to new question templates for known relations with high accuracy and enables zero-shot extraction of unseen relations at lower accuracy.
_summary.methodology: Map each relation slot to natural-language question templates and train a reading-comprehension model (based on BiDAF, extended to model answerability) on large distant-supervision-derived datasets to extract answer spans (or predict no-answer) from entity Wikipedia articles.
limitations: Zero-shot generalization to completely unseen relations yields lower accuracy than for known relations/questions.
A substantial fraction of errors stem from annotation/missing Wikidata entries and inaccurate span selection (e.g., returning too-specific spans), for which the model is penalized.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - relation extraction via reading comprehension

reviewer_a.name: Theoretical Skeptic
reviewer_a.background: Senior researcher in machine learning and NLP with a focus on formal guarantees, robustness, and statistical learning theory (professor-level, 15+ years).
traits: Penalizes papers that make strong generalization/zero-shot claims without formal analysis or provable guarantees (subtracts points if no theoretical justification is provided).
Requires rigorous noise analysis for distantly supervised data: deducts points if fewer than 5% of training examples are human-verified or if no characterization of label noise is provided.

Demands comprehensive controlled baselines and ablations: penalizes papers with fewer than 3 competitive baselines or missing ablations for core design choices (e.g., answerability module, question-template variability).
Reduces score when evaluation lacks task-appropriate difficulty splits (e.g., held-out relations or templates) or when statistical significance testing is absent.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Conservative and rigor-driven: I prioritize formal understanding, robustness to noisy supervision, comprehensive baselines, and careful empirical protocol over purely empirical novelty.

reviewer_b.name: Practical NLP Advocate
reviewer_b.background: Senior applied NLP researcher (industry/academic hybrid) experienced in building large-scale systems and datasets for information extraction and QA, focused on practical utility and reproducibility.
traits: Rewards novel problem formulations and reductions that simplify downstream engineering (gives bonus points for reframing tasks in broadly useful ways, e.g., relation extraction as QA).
Favors scalable, low-cost data-generation methods: positively scores papers that demonstrate large training corpora produced at low monetary cost with reasonable empirical performance.
Credits clear empirical demonstrations of zero-shot or few-shot transfer, even if theoretical analysis is limited (increases score when practical transfer is shown).
Gives extra weight if code, templates, and datasets are released or if the approach is straightforward to reproduce/extend.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Pragmatic and impact-oriented: I value methods that unlock practical scale, low-cost data, and new usable problem formulations, and I am willing to accept limited theory if empirical evidence is strong and reproducible.
